era_context: The late Qin Dynasty under Qin Shi Huang (r. 221–210 BCE), at the height of Qin power and territorial consolidation. The Hellenistic world in the eastern Mediterranean under the Seleucid Empire and Ptolemaic Egypt, following the Fourth Syrian War (219–217 BCE). Rome in the early post-Second Punic War period, consolidating Mediterranean dominance. This year falls within the final year of Qin Shi Huang's reign; his death in 210 BCE would mark a turning point toward dynastic collapse.

events.title: Qin Shi Huang's Final Year of Reign
events.description: In 211 BCE, Qin Shi Huang was in the final year of his reign (he died in 210 BCE). The Qin Empire was at its territorial peak, controlling most of what is now China. The emperor was aging and increasingly preoccupied with immortality and succession planning. According to the Shiji, Qin Shi Huang undertook tours of the empire in his final years, partly to reinforce his authority and partly in search of elixirs of immortality. The year 211 BCE represents the apex of Qin centralized power before the succession crisis and subsequent collapse. Administrative systems were functioning, though tensions were building among the imperial family and court factions regarding succession.
key_figures: Qin Shi Huang (emperor, in final year of life)

sources.name: Qin administrative records and archaeological evidence

events.certainty_note: The general state of the Qin Empire in 211 BCE is well-established. Specific events for this year are not detailed in surviving sources, but the broader context is secure.
cross_references: Led to -210-001 (death of Qin Shi Huang)

events.title: Antiochus III's Military Campaigns in Asia Minor
events.description: In 211 BCE, Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire was engaged in military campaigns to consolidate Seleucid control over Asia Minor and recover territories. Antiochus III (r. 223–187 BCE) is known as 'the Great' for his military successes in expanding and stabilizing the Seleucid realm. In 211 BCE, he was in the midst of his campaigns against rebellious satraps and rival powers in Asia Minor. These campaigns were part of his broader strategy to restore Seleucid power after the losses following Alexander's death and the subsequent Hellenistic wars.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's campaigns in Asia Minor during this period are well-attested in general, but specific events for 211 BCE are not detailed in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Seleucid expansion under Antiochus III

events.title: Roman Military Operations in the Mediterranean
events.description: In 211 BCE, Rome was consolidating its military dominance in the Mediterranean following the Second Punic War (218–201 BCE). Roman legions were engaged in various operations to secure newly conquered territories and suppress resistance. The year 211 BCE falls within the period of Roman expansion and reorganization. Specific military campaigns for this exact year are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the broader context of Roman military activity and consolidation is clear.
events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman military activity in 211 BCE is established, but specific events for this year are not prominently recorded.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman expansion in the Mediterranean

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The characterization of 211 BCE as the final year of Qin Shi Huang's reign is secure, though details of his activities in this specific year are sparse in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 211 BCE is dominated by Chinese sources, primarily the Shiji. The Hellenistic and Roman records are from later historians (Polybius, Livy, Appian) writing over a century after these events. For China, the Shiji provides a narrative framework, but specific events for 211 BCE are not detailed. Archaeological evidence from Qin sites provides some context but limited year-specific information. The narrative is shaped by Han Dynasty perspectives on the Qin. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, Mesoamerica, and Oceania have no known written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Qin Shi Huang's final year led directly to his death in 210 BCE and the succession crisis
Antiochus III's campaigns in Asia Minor and Roman consolidation in the Mediterranean were occurring simultaneously
